---
title: "Object-Oriented Basics"
description: "Define classes, create objects, store attributes, write methods, and use self."
execute:
  echo: true
categories: [object-oriented-python]
order: 420
---
::: {.lesson-meta}
**Level:** Intermediate
**Estimated time:** 35–50 minutes
**You will learn:** Define classes, create objects, store attributes, write methods, and use self.
**Practice in:** VS Code, Jupyter, or Colab
:::

## Why this matters

Define classes, create objects, store attributes, write methods, and use self.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one clear explanation.

::: {.callout-note title="Guiding questions"}
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Object-Oriented Basics** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Learning objectives"}
You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 3 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- **1. Classes and Objects** — Define a new kind of object and create instances from it.
- **2. Attributes and Methods** — Store object data in attributes and object behavior in methods.
- **3. __init__ and self** — Initialize each object with its own starting data.

## Classes, objects, and attributes

::: {.concept-diagram}
A class describes a kind of object. Each object has its own attributes, and
methods use `self` to work with that particular object.

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| eval: true
flowchart LR
  class["class Student<br/>blueprint"] --> ada["object<br/>ada"]
  class --> grace["object<br/>grace"]
  ada --> ada_name["attribute<br/>name = 'Ada'"]
  grace --> grace_name["attribute<br/>name = 'Grace'"]
  ada --> method["method call<br/>ada.study()"]
  method --> self["self refers to<br/>ada"]
```

If `self` feels mysterious, read it as "this specific object."
:::

## 1. Classes and Objects

Define a new kind of object and create instances from it.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
A class is a cookie cutter, and objects are the individual cookies.
:::

### What this means

A class is a blueprint; an object is one concrete instance built from that blueprint.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
class Dog:
    pass

fido = Dog()
print(type(fido))
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `class Dog:` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `pass` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
3. `fido = Dog()` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
4. `print(type(fido))` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
A class describes many possible objects; an instance is one actual object.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "object-oriented-python-classes-and-objects",
  "title": "Classes and Objects checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Classes and Objects?",
      "options": [
        "A class is a blueprint; an object is one concrete instance built from that blueprint.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A class is a blueprint; an object is one concrete instance built from that blueprint."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A class is a cookie cutter, and objects are the individual cookies."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 2. Attributes and Methods

Store object data in attributes and object behavior in methods.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
Attributes are facts on an ID card; methods are actions the person can perform.
:::

### What this means

Attributes describe what an object knows; methods describe what it can do.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
class Counter:
    value = 0

    def show(self):
        print(self.value)

counter = Counter()
counter.show()
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `class Counter:` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `value = 0` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
3. `def show(self):` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
4. `print(self.value)` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
5. `counter = Counter()` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
6. Continue the same process for the remaining lines, one line at a time.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
A method needs self as its first parameter so it can work with the specific object.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "object-oriented-python-attributes-and-methods",
  "title": "Attributes and Methods checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Attributes and Methods?",
      "options": [
        "Attributes describe what an object knows; methods describe what it can do.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Attributes describe what an object knows; methods describe what it can do."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Attributes are facts on an ID card; methods are actions the person can perform."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 3. __init__ and self

Initialize each object with its own starting data.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
__init__ is the sign-up form each new club member fills out; self is the individual
member's record.
:::

### What this means

__init__ runs when a new object is created; self refers to that object.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

student = Student("Ada")
print(student.name)
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `class Student:` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `def __init__(self, name):` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
3. `self.name = name` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
4. `student = Student("Ada")` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
5. `print(student.name)` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
Forgetting self.name stores nothing on the object. name alone is just a local variable.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "object-oriented-python-init-and-self",
  "title": "__init__ and self checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes __init__ and self?",
      "options": [
        "__init__ runs when a new object is created; self refers to that object.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: __init__ runs when a new object is created; self refers to that object."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: __init__ is the sign-up form each new club member fills out; self is the individual member's record."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
first example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.callout-tip title="Key points"}
- **Classes and Objects**: A class is a blueprint; an object is one concrete instance built from that blueprint.
- **Attributes and Methods**: Attributes describe what an object knows; methods describe what it can do.
- **__init__ and self**: __init__ runs when a new object is created; self refers to that object.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python classes tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/classes.html
- dataclasses documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
